result:
The first thing to know is that the sky is not blue; it is simply the color of whatever light is left after the atmosphere has mugged the sun. A planetary hold-up, repeated every dawn, and we call the loot “day.” I keep this fact in my pocket like a matchbook from a bar I never meant to leave, because it reminds me that almost every familiar thing is a subtraction, not a gift.

I was born in the subtractive light of late October, the year the river froze early and the power plant died of a heart attack. My mother says the hospital ran on candle power that night—nurses carrying wax stubs on dinner plates, obstetricians counting pulse by flashlight. I emerged, slick and indignant, into a world already practicing its own absence. The first sound I heard was the hush of generators failing, which may explain my lifelong distrust of anything that hums.

Childhood was a village of four streets, a bus stop, and a bakery that sold yesterday’s bread at half price. My father taught me how to walk by placing me on the white line in the middle of the road and letting the yellow Volvo bear down. “Either you master the perpendicular,” he said, “or you learn the afterlife.” I waddled, screamed, and pitched forward into his arms, thereby discovering both velocity and rescue, two themes I still rotate like coins behind my teeth.

When I was nine the circus arrived with a single elephant named Masha who could paint. They set up a canvas under the lime trees and Masha, swaying like a drunk metronome, swabbed rectangles that looked uncannily like the windows of our school. I studied each brushstroke for a hidden message, some pachyderm Morse that would explain why multiplication tables felt like suffocation. Years later, drunk on cheap wine and unfinished degrees, I found out that Masha had died of tuberculosis in a parking lot outside Zagreb. Her last painting was mostly red. I bought a print online; it hangs above my toilet, warning me that art is not a translation of freedom but a souvenir of confinement.

Adolescence arrived in the form of a geometry teacher who worshipped dead languages and carried a thesaurus like a missal. He once kept us after class to prove, with compass and sorrow, that parallel lines meet at infinity. “Infinity,” he whispered, “is the only place where betrayal does not occur.” I repeated the phrase so often it developed roots under my tongue. Much later, when I learned that he had hanged himself from the basketball hoop, I pictured the rope as a vertical asymptote, forever approaching but never quite reaching the peace implied by zero denominator.

I left town on a train that smelled of coal and wet wool, believing distance was a solvent. Cities accumulated like bruises: Leipzig, Gdańsk, Marseille, every skyline a different shade of bruise-blue. I learned to sleep on night buses, to wash my hair in bar sinks, to conjugate hunger in three languages. Somewhere between a dock strike and sunrise, I met a woman who claimed her left eye saw only the past. She kept it covered with a postage stamp from countries that no longer existed—first Yugoslavia, then Rhodesia, then something she refused to name. When we made love she insisted on total darkness, so I never verified the philatelic allegory. Still, her other eye was future-bright, dilated with tomorrow’s weather, and I loved her the way sailors love sirens: desperately, incorrectly, and with a fatal confidence in the lifeboat of language.

We lived in an attic where pigeons nested in the eaves, their wings beating like unread letters. She taught me how to steal electricity by licking the ends of copper wires and praying; the taste was metallic orgasm, the prayer atheist. Together we cooked rice over candle stubs and argued about whether time was a spiral or a cracked hinge. One night she removed the postage stamp; the eye beneath was milk-white, perfectly blind, yet it wept when I played a certain song on the harmonica. We never spoke of the tears. Six months later she boarded a ship sailing for Montevideo, carrying only a suitcase full of bird feathers. She wrote once: “The sea is a lie that keeps us from dying of literalness.” I have no idea what that means, but I repeat it whenever the wallpaper peels like sunburn.

Middle age arrived disguised as stability: a lease, a job stamping due-date slips at a university library, a houseplant I named Schrödinger because it hovered between life and death with such democratic indecision. I discovered that routine is not the enemy of wonder but its chrysalis; the same tram, the same vending machine coffee, the same janitor humming Piaf at 6:47 a.m.—these became the grid on which I projected private auroras. I learned the names of constellations and then immediately forgot them, preferring my own shapes: a bicycle pump, a misbuttoned coat, the silhouette of Masha painting her red despair. Students came and went, leaving behind graffiti in the margins of returned books—tiny conspiracies of ink. One wrote: “Nothing is true until it has been overdue at least twice.” I promoted him mentally to professor emeritus of my heart.

One winter I grew obsessed with the weight of shadows. I borrowed a precision scale from the physics department and spent nights weighing objects against their own darkness. A candle stub: 2.3 grams. Its shadow: immeasurable, but lighter than the breath I held. The implication was clear: we leave less of ourselves behind than we think. I published nothing, but I stopped fearing insomnia, recognizing it as the mind’s polite refusal to be evicted from the skull before closing time.

Then came the week the city lost its reflection. The river froze matte-black, refusing mirrors. Shop windows showed only the interior of the viewer, a dizzy recursion. People wandered the streets checking their faces in spoons, in puddles of oil, in each other’s sunglasses, finding stranger after stranger. I saw a girl crying at a tram stop because her eyes no longer recognized her eyelids. The trauma was collective but unspoken, like a family secret finally tall enough to reach the light switch. On the final night I climbed the cathedral tower carrying a can of silver paint. Without plan or permit, I graffitied the word REMEMBER onto the copper roof, letters tall enough to be read by planes or angels or whoever audits such accounts. The next morning the thaw began; reflections returned, but they lagged half a second behind real movement, a ghost delay we learned to inhabit. My graffiti remained, oxidizing into a pale scar. No one ever spoke of it, yet sometimes strangers would nod at me in the street, as if we co-owned an unlisted country.

Now I am old in the way a bookshelf is old: upright, dusty, improbably holding. The plant Schrödinger finally collapsed into certainty—dead, I think, though I water it anyway, a kind of vegetative prayer. My left knee predicts rain with an accuracy that shames meteorology. I have stopped weighing shadows; instead I catalog sounds the city no longer makes—the clatter of tram tickets, the hiss of film projectors, the small silence that followed your name spoken in a room now vacant. These absent sounds gather at my window each evening like moths, and I let them in, because absence is just another frequency, and I have decided to become a radio.

Last week I found the matchbook from the sky-is-not-blue bar. The phosphorus is too old to ignite, but if you hold it under running water the cardboard bleeds indigo, a trick I cannot explain. I like to think the universe keeps a tiny ledger where every subtraction is eventually repaid in pigment, in octave, in sudden recollection. Today the sky is the color of my mother’s candles the night I was born. I stand outside, arms open, waiting to be mugged by light, ready to receive whatever remains.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
